overall.summary: The quiz set is strong overall: it is well grounded in the chapter, mostly reasoning-oriented, and appropriate for a foundational chapter at position 5/33. The remaining issues are concentrated in build-up hygiene, a few weak/recall-leaning items, and one clear content error in an ORDER question.

missed_buildup_opportunities.explanation: The answer re-explains memory walls and accelerator preference at some length instead of assuming familiarity with the prior-chapter term. The question could lean more on applying the already-known concept to neural computation.
The answer re-explains latency/power/memory-reuse distinctions in a fairly generic way. Since these are established earlier in the book, the item could be tighter and more chapter-specific.

forward_reference_violations.explanation: The FILL answer names 'feature engineering' as if the term is already established, but it does not appear in the provided prior_vocab list and is first taught in this chapter's section text. This is not fatal, but it means the item is pure in-section term recall rather than build-up use.

build_up.chapter_level_assessment: The chapter generally does leverage prior context well, especially the iron law, memory wall, workflow, and deployment framing. It does not feel isolated, but several questions still behave as if they must re-explain known systems ideas instead of confidently building on them.

distribution.type_mix_assessment: slightly over-MCQ, slightly under-SHORT; TF/FILL/ORDER are within reasonable bounds for a single chapter

per_question_issues.description: This FILL is essentially direct term recall for 'feature engineering.' The surrounding sentence points almost uniquely to the textbook term, so it tests naming more than systems reasoning or inference.
per_question_issues.suggested_fix: Replace it with a SHORT or MCQ that asks what systems limitation feature engineering imposed compared with deep learning, using the HOG/SIFT/Gabor examples and compute trade-offs.

per_question_issues.description: The ORDER answer appears wrong relative to the section's own causal story. The chapter presents data availability, algorithmic innovations, and computing infrastructure as a mutually reinforcing cycle, but when it narrates emergence it repeatedly states that data abundance and algorithmic advances together created demand for stronger infrastructure; putting infrastructure last as a linear order is shaky, and the explanation overcommits to a causal sequence the text does not clearly endorse.
per_question_issues.suggested_fix: Either convert this to an MCQ/SHORT about the reinforcing cycle, or if keeping ORDER, use a genuinely sequential process from the section rather than cyclical factors.

per_question_issues.description: The FILL for 'transistor tax' is more nomenclature recall than conceptual inference. The important takeaway is the hardware cost asymmetry between ReLU and sigmoid/tanh, not the coined label itself.
per_question_issues.suggested_fix: Turn this into an MCQ or SHORT asking why activation choice affects silicon area/energy and what consequence that has for practical hidden-layer design.

per_question_issues.description: This True/False is straightforward once the reader sees the two terms together; it reads close to a terminology distinction rather than a misconception likely to trap a reader.
per_question_issues.suggested_fix: Make it scenario-based, e.g., ask whether calling optimizer.step() without backprop is enough to learn, or whether gradients alone update parameters.

per_question_issues.description: The statement is true and nearly restates a central claim of the section. It checks recognition of a prominent point rather than deeper deployment reasoning.
per_question_issues.suggested_fix: Rewrite as an MCQ or SHORT comparing two deployment memory plans, asking which one inference can exploit and why.

per_question_issues.description: The TF item is essentially a direct restatement of the section's main thesis about USPS illustrating D·A·M alignment. It does not probe much beyond recall of the summary framing.
per_question_issues.suggested_fix: Replace with a scenario asking which D, A, or M axis is misaligned in a concrete failure case, or why maximizing only one axis fails.

per_question_issues.description: The FILL for 'overfitting' is conceptually important but still functions as direct vocabulary recall; the description practically defines the term verbatim.
per_question_issues.suggested_fix: Ask instead how an engineer would detect overfitting from training vs. validation behavior, or what intervention follows from the observed gap.

per_question_issues.description: This TF is very easy because the section repeatedly emphasizes end-to-end pipeline performance and explicitly uses USPS as evidence. The falsehood is obvious to a careful reader.
per_question_issues.suggested_fix: Upgrade to an application item asking which pipeline stage is the likely bottleneck in a given deployment scenario even when model accuracy is high.
